title: Video Recommendation System - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a personalized homepage recommendation system using hybrid candidate generation and ranking.
problem: Build a homepage video recommendation system for a YouTube-like product with global users, multilingual videos, 10 billion videos, interaction-based training data, and a 200 ms serving budget.

functional: Handle global users and multilingual videos.
Support new users and new videos.
Measure recommendation quality online and offline.
requirements.designPrompt: Keep the recommendation stack clear: data logging, candidate generation, ranking, serving cache, exploration/cold start, and evaluation.

capacity.label: Candidate count
capacity.value: Generate hundreds to thousands, rank tens to hundreds.

capacity.label: Serving latency
capacity.value: Most expensive retrieval and feature work must be precomputed or cached.

capacity.label: Objective
capacity.value: Blend watch time, satisfaction, explicit feedback, and guardrails.

capacity.label: Cold start
capacity.value: Use content features, trends, locale, and exploration.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: 10 billion videos in the corpus. Recommendations are personalized for logged-in homepage users. Interaction logs include impressions, clicks, watch time, completion, likes, and hides. Serving target is under 200 ms.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: At 10B videos, scoring everything online is impossible. The design must use retrieval plus ranking, with precomputed features and candidate caches.

dataModel.name: video
dataModel.note: Recommendable video metadata and content features.

steps.title: 1. Define Recommendation Objective
steps.description: The goal is sustained user engagement and satisfaction, not just clicks. The objective should combine watch behavior, explicit feedback, and guardrails against clickbait.

concepts.definition: The target used to order videos for users.
concepts.whyItMatters: It shapes all model behavior.

concepts.definition: How long users watch after recommendation.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is useful but can overfavor long videos.

concepts.definition: A metric that prevents optimizing one signal at user expense.
concepts.whyItMatters: It helps avoid clickbait and low-quality engagement.

deepDives: Pure completion can overfavor short videos; pure watch time can overfavor long videos.
bottlenecks: Bad objective design causes bad recommendations at scale.

view.caption: A user on the homepage generates interaction events that feed the engagement-and-satisfaction objective the system optimizes for.

options.name: Long-term satisfaction objective (default)
options.description: The ranker optimizes a blended objective combining watch behavior, explicit feedback (likes, surveys), and anti-clickbait guardrails rather than a single click signal.
pros: Optimizes for watch-time plus satisfaction signals (likes, surveys, low skip/dislike), aligning with retention.
Resists clickbait that wins clicks but disappoints, protecting long-term engagement.
Combines multiple outcome signals into one objective the ranker can learn.
cons: Satisfaction signals are sparse and delayed, making the label harder to learn.
Multi-objective weighting is subjective and needs ongoing tuning and guardrails.
Harder to attribute and explain than a single click target.

view.caption: Homepage interaction events feed a blended engagement-and-satisfaction objective combining watch, explicit feedback, and anti-clickbait guardrails.

options.name: Click-through rate objective
options.description: The model optimizes the probability a user clicks a recommendation, a dense and immediate label that is easy to log and evaluate.
pros: Clicks are dense, immediate, and easy to log, so the model trains quickly with lots of signal.
Simple, well-understood label that's straightforward to evaluate offline.
Strong short-term engagement lift.
cons: Rewards clickbait thumbnails/titles that get the click but leave users unsatisfied.
Ignores how long or whether the video was actually watched.
Can degrade long-term retention while looking good on CTR dashboards.

view.caption: Homepage interaction events feed a click-through objective that optimizes the probability the user clicks a recommendation.

options.name: Pure watch-time objective
options.description: The model optimizes total time watched as a single target, treating minutes viewed as the proxy for value delivered.
pros: Directly optimizes total time watched, a strong proxy for value delivered.
Denser than satisfaction surveys and harder to game than a click.
Simple single target that correlates with session length.
cons: Can over-favor long videos and autoplay loops regardless of user benefit.
May promote sensational or addictive content that maximizes minutes, not satisfaction.
Misses explicit dissatisfaction signals like dislikes and 'not interested'.

view.caption: Homepage interaction events feed a watch-time objective that optimizes total minutes viewed as the single target.

steps.title: 2. Build Interaction Logs and Features
steps.description: The system logs impressions and outcomes, then builds user, video, creator, language, and context features for training and serving.
steps.decisionPrompt: What data is needed to train and serve personalized recommendations?

concepts.definition: A record that a video was shown.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents training only on clicked positives.

concepts.definition: A vector summarizing user preferences.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports collaborative and content matching.

concepts.definition: A vector summarizing video content and audience behavior.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets recommendation models compare videos and users.

deepDives: Features must be available offline for training and online for serving.
talkingPoints: Separate user features from video features.

view.caption: The homepage service writes impression and watch logs, which feed feature pipelines that build user features and video features.

steps.title: 3. Generate Candidates With Collaborative Signals
steps.description: Candidate generation retrieves videos watched by similar users, videos co-watched with recent history, subscriptions, trends, and content-similar items.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we find a manageable set of plausible videos from 10 billion?

concepts.definition: Using behavior of similar users or items.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures taste patterns not obvious from metadata.

concepts.definition: A fast recall-oriented retrieval stage.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes ranking feasible.

concepts.definition: One retrieval strategy contributing videos.
concepts.whyItMatters: Multiple sources improve coverage.

deepDives: Candidate generation maximizes recall; ranking will sort and filter.

view.caption: The candidate service queries collaborative, subscription, trending, and content-similar sources, unioning their results into one candidate set for the user.

options.name: Collaborative filtering retrieval (default)
options.description: Candidates are retrieved from co-engagement patterns ('people who watched X also watched Y') as nearest-neighbor lookups over learned user/item embeddings.
pros: Co-engagement patterns surface videos liked by similar users without needing content understanding.
Captures latent taste that content features miss ('people who watch X also watch Y').
Mature, cheap to precompute as nearest-neighbor lookups over learned embeddings.
cons: Cold-start: brand-new users and videos have no interaction history to draw on.
Popularity bias — heavily-watched items dominate, crowding out the long tail.
Needs a separate path for fresh content (handled later by exploration).

view.caption: The candidate service draws mainly from collaborative co-engagement candidates (plus subscription, trending, similar) into the candidate set.

options.name: Content-based retrieval
options.description: Candidates are retrieved by matching video content features (topic, audio, text embeddings) to the user's history, so even brand-new videos are reachable.
pros: Recommends from video features (topic, audio, text, embeddings), so new videos are reachable on day one.
No reliance on co-engagement, sidestepping the item cold-start problem.
Explainable: 'similar to what you just watched' maps to concrete features.
cons: Tends to over-specialize, creating a filter bubble of near-identical content.
Misses serendipitous cross-topic taste that collaborative signals capture.
Quality depends entirely on the richness of content features.

view.caption: The candidate service matches the user's history against content features to retrieve content-similar candidates, reaching even brand-new videos.

options.name: Two-tower embedding ANN retrieval
options.description: A user tower and a video tower are learned into a shared embedding space, and retrieval is approximate-nearest-neighbor lookup over a vector index of all videos.
pros: Learns user and video towers into a shared embedding space; retrieval is fast approximate-nearest-neighbor.
Blends collaborative and content signals into one model, mitigating both cold-start and filter bubbles.
Scales to retrieve from 10B videos in milliseconds via a vector index.
cons: Training and serving two towers plus an ANN index is heavier infrastructure.
Embeddings must be refreshed as content and tastes drift, adding pipelines.
Harder to debug why a given video was retrieved.

view.caption: The candidate service embeds the user via a two-tower model and retrieves nearest videos from an ANN vector index into the candidate set.

steps.title: 4. Rank Candidates With Content and User Features
steps.description: The ranker predicts satisfaction for each user-video pair using collaborative, content, creator, freshness, language, and context features.
steps.decisionPrompt: What should the ranking model know about the user and each candidate video?

concepts.definition: Combining collaborative and content-based signals.
concepts.whyItMatters: It works better for both known tastes and new content.
concepts.group: Rank Candidates With Content and User Features

concepts.definition: Training a model to order candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: It optimizes the final homepage order.
concepts.group: Rank Candidates With Content and User Features

concepts.definition: A feature expressing interaction between user and item attributes.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures match quality such as user language versus video language.
concepts.group: Rank Candidates With Content and User Features

deepDives: The ranking model should predict multiple outcomes or a calibrated blended score rather than only click probability.
bottlenecks: Ranking too many candidates breaches latency.

view.caption: The candidate set is joined with features and scored by the ranking model into per-video scores.

options.name: Deep neural ranker (default)
options.description: A deep neural network scores each user-video pair over collaborative, content, and context features, consuming high-cardinality embeddings and predicting multiple outcomes jointly.
pros: Learns nonlinear interactions among user, video, and context features for the engagement objective.
Naturally consumes high-cardinality embeddings (video id, channel, watch history).
Can be multi-task, predicting watch-time, like, and skip jointly.
cons: Training and low-latency serving need GPU/accelerator infrastructure and a model server.
Less interpretable, so debugging a bad ranking is harder.
Heavier to retrain and roll out than a tree model.

view.caption: The feature join feeds a deep neural ranker that scores each user-video pair over collaborative, content, and context features into video scores.

options.name: Gradient-boosted trees (GBDT)
options.description: A gradient-boosted tree model scores candidates over dense tabular features, training and serving cheaply on CPU with interpretable feature importances.
pros: Strong accuracy on dense tabular features with far less tuning than a deep net.
Cheap to train and serve on CPU — low latency without accelerators.
Feature importances make it relatively interpretable for debugging.
cons: Handles high-cardinality ids and embeddings poorly, limiting personalization depth.
Doesn't learn raw content embeddings the way a neural model can.
Harder to express multi-task objectives in one model.

view.caption: The feature join feeds a gradient-boosted tree ranker that scores candidates over dense tabular features into video scores.

options.name: Two-stage: cheap retrieval rank + heavy rerank
options.description: A lightweight model scores all candidates first, then an expensive model reranks only the top-k, keeping per-request compute within the latency budget.
pros: A lightweight model scores all candidates, then an expensive model reranks only the top-k.
Keeps the per-request compute within the 200 ms budget at large candidate counts.
Lets you use a richer model where it matters most (the final ordering).
cons: Two models to train, evaluate, and keep consistent.
The cheap first stage can prune a great candidate before the heavy stage ever sees it.
More serving complexity and more places for skew between stages.

view.caption: The feature join feeds a lightweight ranker that scores all candidates, then a heavy reranker reorders only the top-k into video scores.

steps.title: 5. Assemble a Hybrid Serving Pipeline
steps.description: The online request gets cached candidates, fetches fresh features, scores in batch, applies rules, hydrates video cards, and returns the homepage.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which work belongs online and which work should be precomputed?

concepts.definition: Precomputed candidates stored for users or cohorts.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces request-time retrieval cost.

concepts.definition: Scoring many candidates in one model call.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lowers model serving overhead.

concepts.definition: Business and product constraints after scoring.
concepts.whyItMatters: They enforce diversity, freshness, and safety guardrails.

flows.name: Homepage request
flows.note: Serving uses precomputed candidates and online ranking to stay inside the latency budget.

deepDives: Precomputing candidates does not mean final ranking is stale; the ranker can use fresh features online.
bottlenecks: Candidate cache misses for new or inactive users.

view.caption: The home API pulls cached candidates and online features, runs the ranker, applies rules and diversity, hydrates video metadata, and returns the page to the client.

options.name: Hybrid: precompute candidates, rank online (default)
options.description: Candidate generation and embeddings are precomputed and cached, and the online request only fetches fresh features and runs the cheaper ranking pass.
pros: Candidate generation and embeddings are precomputed and cached, so the request only does the cheap online rank.
Online ranking still uses fresh context (time, device, last action) for personalization.
Balances freshness against the 200 ms budget — the heavy retrieval is off the hot path.
cons: Cached candidates can be slightly stale between precompute cycles.
Two systems (batch precompute + online serving) to keep in sync.
Cache invalidation and warm-up add operational complexity.

view.caption: The home API reads precomputed candidates from the cache and fresh online features, runs the ranker, applies rules, and hydrates metadata.

options.name: Fully online (compute everything per request)
options.description: Every request retrieves candidates and ranks them live with no precompute or cache, so it always uses the freshest signals along one serving path.
pros: Always uses the freshest signals — no staleness from precomputed candidates.
One serving path, no batch/cache layer to operate or invalidate.
Reacts immediately to a user's just-now actions.
cons: Retrieving from 10B videos and ranking per request blows the 200 ms latency budget.
Massive per-request compute cost at scale.
A traffic spike hits the most expensive path directly with no caching cushion.

view.caption: The home API retrieves candidates online per request, ranks them, and applies rules, using the freshest signals with no precompute.

options.name: Fully precomputed recommendation lists
options.description: A batch job computes and stores a full ranked list per user, so serving is a single cache read with no online retrieval or ranking.
pros: Per-user lists are computed in batch and stored, so serving is a single fast cache read.
Trivially meets the latency budget regardless of model complexity.
Predictable, cheap serving cost.
cons: Recommendations are stale until the next batch run — no reaction to in-session behavior.
Storing full ranked lists for every user is expensive at scale.
Can't incorporate live context like current device or time of day.

view.caption: The home API serves a single read from the precomputed-lists cache that a batch pipeline writes per user, with no online retrieval or ranking.

steps.title: 6. Meet the 200 ms Latency Budget
steps.description: Use offline batch features for stable signals, nearline updates for recent behavior, online caches for hot users, and bounded candidate counts for ranking.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does the system avoid expensive computation during the request?

concepts.definition: A feature computed periodically over large data.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is cheap to serve but less fresh.

concepts.definition: A feature updated from streams soon after events occur.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures recent interests.

concepts.definition: A maximum number of candidates scored online.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects latency.

deepDives: A 200 ms target forces most graph mining and embedding work out of the request path.
talkingPoints: Mention fallback recommendations when features are missing.

view.caption: Batch pipelines and nearline streams populate the feature store, which fills a serving cache the home API and ranker read within the latency budget.

steps.title: 7. Handle Cold Start and Exploration
steps.description: New users can receive locale, trend, and onboarding-based recommendations. New videos can be explored through content features and controlled traffic allocation.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do recommendations work when little interaction data exists?

concepts.definition: The lack of historical data for a user or item.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is a central recommender-system challenge.

concepts.definition: Showing some uncertain items to learn their quality.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gathers data beyond current model preferences.

concepts.definition: Avoiding too many similar recommendations.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves discovery and reduces repetition.

deepDives: Exploration should be budgeted, not random chaos across the whole homepage.
bottlenecks: Too much exploration harms short-term engagement.
Too little exploration starves new creators.
talkingPoints: Use content-based ranking for new videos before collaborative signals exist.

view.caption: New users get regional trends and new videos surface via content features, both feeding the exploration allocator that places them on the homepage.

options.name: Content + popularity bootstrap (default)
options.description: New users get regional and trending popular videos while new videos surface via content features, reusing existing trending and content sources with zero history.
pros: New users get regional/trending popular videos; new videos surface via content features — both work with zero history.
Safe, low-risk recommendations while interaction data accumulates.
Reuses the content and trending sources already in the system.
cons: Popularity is generic and not yet personalized, so early experience is bland.
Doesn't actively gather the targeted feedback needed to learn the new user/video fast.
Trending can crowd out genuinely relevant niche items.

view.caption: Regional trends seed new users and content features surface new videos, both routed through the exploration allocator onto the homepage.

options.name: Bandit exploration (Thompson / UCB)
options.description: A multi-armed bandit deliberately allocates impressions to uncertain items, balancing exploration against exploitation to learn their value with minimal regret.
pros: Deliberately allocates impressions to uncertain items to learn their value fast, minimizing regret.
Gives new videos a fair, principled shot at an audience instead of relying on popularity.
Continuously balances exploration against exploiting known-good recommendations.
cons: Showing exploratory items risks short-term engagement dips that need guardrails.
More complex to implement, tune, and monitor than a popularity bootstrap.
Needs careful attribution to credit exploration with the long-term learning gain.

view.caption: A bandit allocator takes uncertain new users and items and allocates homepage impressions to balance exploration and exploitation.

options.name: Onboarding interest survey
options.description: New users are asked to pick topics and creators during onboarding, giving an explicit personalization signal from the first session.
pros: Explicitly asks new users to pick topics/creators, giving an instant personalization signal.
Sidesteps the cold-start data gap with declared preferences from minute one.
Simple to build and easy for the user to understand.
cons: Adds onboarding friction that some users skip or abandon.
Stated interests often differ from actual watch behavior.
Does nothing for new-video cold start — only addresses the user side.

view.caption: New users pick topics in an interest survey that seeds their homepage preferences from the first session.

steps.title: 8. Evaluate and Operate Recommendation Models
steps.description: Offline metrics, replay evaluation, and online experiments govern rollout. Monitoring tracks latency, engagement, satisfaction, diversity, and model/data drift.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we know a recommendation model is safe to launch?

concepts.definition: Testing a model on held-out historical data.
concepts.whyItMatters: It filters bad candidates before live traffic.

concepts.definition: A controlled rollout to real users.
concepts.whyItMatters: It measures actual product impact.

concepts.definition: A decline caused by changing data or user behavior.
concepts.whyItMatters: It tells when retraining or rollback is needed.

deepDives: Guardrails should include hides, quick abandons, diversity, latency, and creator ecosystem health, not only watch time.
bottlenecks: Feedback loops can overfit to what the current recommender already shows.
talkingPoints: Close by explaining the training-serving feedback loop.

view.caption: The trainer's model passes offline evaluation, then an A/B experiment whose metrics and guardrails gate the rollout controller and feed the drift monitor.

functional.how: Candidate generators produce plausible videos, the ranker scores user-video pairs, and post-processing returns a diverse homepage.
Impressions, clicks, watch time, likes, hides, and completions feed feature pipelines and model evaluation.
Candidate generation and caches avoid scoring the whole corpus, while bounded batch inference keeps request latency under control.
New users and new videos fall back to content features, regional trends, locale, subscriptions, and controlled exploration.

nonFunctional.how: Precomputed candidates, online feature stores, batch model serving, and metadata hydration budgets protect the 200 ms target.
Offline evaluation, A/B experiments, guardrail metrics, and diversity rules prevent pure clickbait optimization.
Nearline features and exploration budgets help the system learn recent interests and give new videos measured exposure.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a hybrid recommender with offline learning and online ranking. Interaction logs build user and video features, candidate generators retrieve plausible videos from collaborative and content sources, candidate caches reduce request cost, the online ranker scores bounded candidates within 200 ms, rules handle diversity and exploration, and experiments control model rollout.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through a homepage recommendation request, then explain how impressions and watch outcomes improve the next model version.

concepts.definition: Collaborative signals and content signals work together.
concepts.whyItMatters: It handles mature users, new videos, multilingual content, and cold start better than either alone.

concepts.definition: Precomputed candidate sets for users or cohorts.
concepts.whyItMatters: It moves expensive retrieval out of the 200 ms request path.

concepts.definition: A controlled slice of recommendations used to learn about uncertain videos.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents the system from starving new creators or overfitting old preferences.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

view.caption: Candidate generators fill the cache; the home API fetches online features, runs the ranking service and exploration with diversity/safety rules, hydrates metadata to the page, and logs feed feature pipelines, training, eval, experiments, and rollout.

options.title: Cold-start heavy variant
options.description: Useful for anonymous or new users, but weaker than the hybrid design once collaborative interaction history exists.

view.caption: The home API pulls trending videos and content-matcher candidates, scores them with a lightweight ranker, hydrates video metadata back to the client, and logs interactions.

flows.name: Homepage serving
flows.note: The online request reads cached candidates, joins fresh features, scores in batch, applies rules, and returns a page.

flows.name: Learning loop
flows.note: Impressions and watch outcomes update features and train candidate and ranking models.

deepDives: The recommender answer is incomplete without both candidate generation and ranking. The 200 ms budget is what forces the split.
bottlenecks: Feedback loops can overexpose already popular videos.
talkingPoints: Avoid pure click, pure completion, or pure watch-time objectives.
Use cold-start fallbacks for new users and videos.
Measure satisfaction and guardrails in experiments.

view.caption: Logs build user/video features; candidate generators (collaborative, subscription, similar, trending) fill the candidate cache; the home API fetches online features, runs the ranker and exploration with rules, hydrates metadata to the page, and logs feed training, offline eval, experiments, and rollout.

followUps: How would you tune exploration safely?
How do you avoid recommending harmful clickbait?
How do you support multilingual recommendations?
What happens when the feature store is unavailable?

nodes.description: User Feature Store: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Video Feature Store
nodes.description: Video Feature Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Video Metadata
nodes.description: Video Metadata: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Watch Outcome Logs
nodes.description: Watch Outcome Logs: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, dashboard, or audit component.
